section.title: Overview
section.para: The nats_consumer module turns &osips; into a pull-mode NATS JetStream consumer. A dedicated module process subscribes to JetStream streams, populates a per-handle SHM ring, and exposes the messages to &osips; routing scripts via nats_fetch() and nats_fetch_batch(). Acknowledgements (nats_ack(), nats_ack_next(), nats_ack_progress(), nats_nak(), nats_term()) travel back to the consumer process over an IPC socket and are then relayed to NATS.
nats_consumer is the receive-side counterpart to the two other &osips; NATS modules:

listitem.para: nats_consumer — pulls messages off JetStream streams and delivers them to routing scripts, with explicit ack and request/reply support.

section.para: Handles are registered dynamically at runtime via the MI command nats_consumer_bind or the script function nats_consumer_bind() (called from startup_route). There are no bind-time modparams: the module loads with an empty registry and every consumer handle is added on demand. When persist_handles is enabled, the registry is snapshotted to disk and rehydrated across restarts.

The module shares the same nats.c connection pool as event_nats (via lib/nats/libnats_pool.so), so a single &osips; instance consumes one NATS connection per worker regardless of which NATS modules are loaded.

section.title: Process Model and Failure Handling
section.para: nats_consumer registers a single dedicated worker named [NATS consumer] whose only job is to drive the JetStream pull loop and hand messages to the SHM ring. Acks travel back over a Unix-domain IPC socket (nats_ack_ipc.c).

If the consumer worker dies for any reason (signal, segfault, OOM kill) the &osips; core treats it as a fatal child death and shuts the entire instance down — exactly the same path a SIP UDP receiver crash takes (signals.c:handle_sigs, SIGCHLD branch). There is no per-process auto-restart; the consumer is not registered with the OSS_PROC_SELFEXIT flag.

Operationally this means the supervisor that runs &osips; (systemd, Kubernetes, etc.) is responsible for restarting the whole &osips; instance after a consumer death. Bound handles persist across that restart only when persist_handles is enabled (see the modparam below); otherwise the registry must be repopulated by re-issuing nats_consumer_bind. In-flight messages whose ack didn't reach NATS before the restart will be re-delivered after ack_wait.

section.title: When you need this module / when you don't

listitem.para: you need explicit-ack JetStream pull-mode consumption from script (nats_fetch, nats_ack, nats_nak, nats_term, nats_ack_progress);
you need fine-grained per-handle counters and a watchdog endpoint (nats_consumer_health).

section.para: You don't need it when: plain fire-and-forget core-NATS subscription with event_route[] dispatch is sufficient — that path is handled by event_nats's subscribe modparam. The event_nats subscribe path has no script-visible ack, no durable consumer state, no replay, and no per-message flow control, but is also a much smaller config and one fewer dedicated process. If your handler can tolerate at-most-once-effective semantics from EVI dispatch, prefer that path.

section.title: &osips; Modules
section.para: Mandatory: at least one other NATS module that calls nats_pool_register() — typically event_nats — must be loaded before nats_consumer. The consumer module does not own the shared lib/nats pool's transport configuration; it consumes the connection set up by the registrant. Without a registrant, the consumer process aborts at child_init with NATS pool: not registered (call nats_pool_register first). Recommended load order: event_nats first, nats_consumer second.
When TLS is used to reach the NATS cluster, load tls_openssl before the first NATS module so the nats.c library defers OpenSSL lifecycle management to &osips;.

Once event_nats and nats_consumer are both loaded, they share one nats.c connection pool and do not contend for the same socket or subject space. cachedb_nats may also be loaded alongside; the same pool is shared three ways.

listitem.para: nats.c v3.13 or later (libnats.so) — the official C client library for NATS. The multi-subject filter and pull-consumer APIs used here require v3.13 at minimum.
A JetStream-enabled NATS server (v2.9 or later).

section.title: persist_handles (integer)
section.para: Opt-in snapshot persistence for the handle registry. When set to 1, every successful nats_consumer_bind or nats_consumer_unbind schedules a debounced (500 ms) write of the full registry as a JSON array to persist_path. On mod_init, the module rehydrates from that file so handles registered by a previous run come back automatically.
A persistence failure at init (missing parent directory, unwritable target, malformed JSON) is logged as a warning and the module loads with an empty registry — it never blocks startup.
Default value: 0 (disabled)

example.title: Enable handle persistence
example.programlisting: modparam("nats_consumer", "persist_handles", 1) modparam("nats_consumer", "persist_path", "/var/lib/opensips/nats_consumer/handles.json")

section.title: persist_path (string)
section.para: Filesystem path used by the persistence writer when persist_handles is 1. The parent directory must exist at init time; the module does not create it. Writes use the standard tempfile + fsync + rename pattern so a crash cannot leave the file half-written.

On rehydrate, the file is rejected with an LM_ERR if it exceeds 10 MiB (NATS_PERSIST_MAX_FILE_BYTES) -- a guard against a misconfigured path or a symlink to a large file (e.g., a log) that would otherwise be slurped entirely into heap before parsing.
Numeric fields that exceed double precision (start_seq above 2^53, start_time_ns in nanoseconds since epoch) are serialized as JSON strings so they round-trip losslessly through the snapshot.

example.title: Override snapshot location
example.programlisting: modparam("nats_consumer", "persist_path", "/etc/opensips/nats_handles.json")

section.title: fetch_batch (integer)
section.para: Module-global default for the JetStream pull-Fetch batch size used by the dedicated consumer process when calling natsSubscription_Fetch. Each handle may override this with a per-handle fetch_batch= bind key.

Range: 1..4096. Larger batches reduce the number of pull requests the consumer process issues against the broker (lower pulls_requested in MI counters, lower broker CPU per consumer). The consumer process clamps the request to the ring's currently-free slot count before each natsSubscription_Fetch call, so a fetch_batch that exceeds the worker's transient drain rate no longer triggers ring-full defer-drops; the broker simply holds the un-fetched messages until the next loop iteration. Pair with a generous ring_capacity (>= 4 * fetch_batch is the recommended ratio) and max_ack_pending (>= ring_capacity) so the clamp has room to deliver full batches.
The drain pattern in the script matters more than this knob alone. Reference numbers, single-instance aarch64 loopback at N=100 000:

listitem.para: nats_fetch+nats_ack per-message: ~2 000 msgs/sec, gated by the OpenSIPS script-interpreter overhead.
nats_fetch_batch +nats_batch_select +nats_ack at fetch_batch=10: ~9 800 msgs/sec.
Same pattern at fetch_batch=64: ~37 500 msgs/sec.
Same pattern at fetch_batch=256: ~89 000 msgs/sec.

section.para: See the README's "Choosing a drain pattern" and "Tuning fetch_batch" sections for the full trade-off matrix. At very high batches (>= 256) the broker's max_ack_pending becomes the next bottleneck; raising it (with ring_capacity raised to match) lifts the ceiling further at the cost of broker memory.

example.title: High-throughput durable -- override fetch_batch + ring

section.title: fetch_timeout_ms (integer)
section.para: Module-global default for the per-Fetch upper-bound wait, in milliseconds. Each handle may override this with a per-handle fetch_timeout_ms= bind key.

Range: 1..60000 (1 ms .. 60 s). Lower values reduce shutdown latency (the consumer process polls this timeout between checking for retire/shutdown signals) at the cost of more wakeups on quiet streams. Higher values reduce idle CPU on streams that rarely have messages, at the cost of slower shutdown.

example.title: Latency-sensitive RPC handle, short timeout

section.title: allow_sync_anywhere (integer)
section.para: Opt-in switch that widens the route mask of the synchronous nats_request entry point so it may be called from SIP-worker contexts (REQUEST_ROUTE, FAILURE_ROUTE, BRANCH_ROUTE, ERROR_ROUTE). When unset (the default), the OpenSIPS parser rejects any call to bare nats_request from those routes with the diagnostic Command <nats_request> cannot be used in the block; only STARTUP_ROUTE, TIMER_ROUTE, LOCAL_ROUTE, EVENT_ROUTE, and ONREPLY_ROUTE remain accepted.

The guard exists because natsConnection_RequestMsg blocks the calling worker for up to timeout_ms. A SIP UDP/TCP worker that blocks on the RPC cannot read its next packet for that long, which on a production proxy with a steady inbound rate quickly stalls SIP throughput. Operators who know their deployment can absorb the worst case (low RPS, a thin SIP gateway in front, generous worker pool sizing) may opt in to the simpler synchronous ergonomics by setting this to 1; the setter callback widens the nats_request entry's flag word to ALL_ROUTES at the moment the modparam directive is parsed, before any route block is checked.

Per-route blocking consequences. Each of the newly-opened routes has its own distinct operational blast radius (verified against the core invocation paths cited below). The setter callback emits an LM_WARN at config-parse time spelling these out so an operator who flips the switch cannot miss them:

listitem.para: REQUEST_ROUTE. Runs on the SIP worker that received the request (receive.c: run_top_route(sroutes->request[DEFAULT_RT])). The worker cannot read its next SIP packet for the duration; under sustained load the recv backlog grows on this worker's interface.

FAILURE_ROUTE. Has two invocation paths with different blast radius and different async support:
Negative-reply trigger -- when an upstream branch returns a final non-2xx, the SIP worker that received the reply runs the failure route inline (modules/tm/t_reply.c: run_failure_handlers). Blast radius identical to REQUEST_ROUTE blocking on that worker; async() IS available from this trigger path because a SIP worker has a full reactor.
fr_timer / fr_inv_timer expiry -- the failure route runs inline on the timer ticker process, which is the single-threaded process forked by start_timer_processes in timer.c (loop body: run_timer_process, a bare select(0,0,0,0,&tv) tick driver with no reactor). This process exists solely to fire the C-level callbacks registered via register_timer() -- including TM's final_response_handler, which in turn calls fake_reply -> relay_reply -> run_failure_handlers inline. Blocking it queues every other transaction's fr_timer fire, every other module's tick callback (retransmissions, watchdogs, etc.) until the block clears. Worse blast radius than blocking a single SIP worker, and async() is not available from this trigger path because the ticker has no reactor -- there is no non-blocking option in this module for fr_timer-triggered failure_route today.

BRANCH_ROUTE. Runs on the SIP worker that is fanning out the transaction's outbound branches (modules/tm/t_fwd.c: pre_print_uac_request). The per-branch INVITE/REGISTER send is delayed by up to timeout_ms; subsequent branches in the same transaction also wait.

ERROR_ROUTE. Runs on the SIP worker that hit a parse error on an inbound request (receive.c) or that tripped an in-action error (action.c: run_error_route). Blocking delays the error response back to the peer.

section.para: Note: timer_route { ... } blocks (not to be confused with the C-level register_timer() callbacks described under FAILURE_ROUTE above) execute on a separate Timer handler process that has a full reactor and supports async(). Calls to bare nats_request from timer_route are accepted by the default mask (TIMER_ROUTE is in the safe allow-list) and async() works there too. The ticker-process limitation discussed under FAILURE_ROUTE / fr_timer is a different process and a different code path.

The recommended pattern for any worker route is the async form, which yields on a per-call eventfd while the reply is in flight and does not block SIP processing: async(nats_request(subj, payload, tmo), reply_route) The async form is accepted from every route that supports async() regardless of this modparam -- request_route, branch_route, error_route, timer_route, event_route, onreply_route, and the reply-triggered branch of failure_route. The only worker-blocking situation without a non-blocking alternative is failure_route entered through fr_timer / fr_inv_timer expiry; lifting that requires an upstream change in tm that dispatches fr_timer expiry through a Timer handler instead of running it inline on the ticker. See nats_request below.
Range: 0 (default, safer) or 1 (opt-in, permissive). Any other value is rejected at config-parse time so a typo cannot silently disarm the guard.

example.title: Opt in -- low-RPS deployment that prefers sync ergonomics
Default -- async from request_route, sync only in startup

section.title: request_id_header (string)
section.para: Outbound header name used to carry the per-call UUIDv7 correlation id auto- minted by both the sync and async nats_request start paths. The id is also exposed to the script via the $nats_request_id pvar and persists across an async() yield so the resume route can read it back for log / trace correlation.

Per-call cost. One clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME) + one getrandom(GRND_NONBLOCK) of 10 bytes per request, plus a 36-byte snprintf. Measured at roughly 200 ns on aarch64; below the noise floor of the libnats round-trip.

UUIDv7 layout. RFC 9562 §5.7 lexicographically-sortable Type-7 UUID: 48-bit unix milliseconds, 4-bit version (7), 12 random bits, 2 variant bits (10b), 62 random bits. Globally unique across instances and time; grep-able as one string from OpenSIPS logs through the NATS subject to the responder's logs.

Auto-stage behaviour. When the modparam is non-empty, every nats_request call stages <header-name>: <uuid> onto the outbound natsMsg iff the script has not already staged the same header explicitly via nats_hdr_set. A deliberate nats_hdr_set("X-Request-Id", "my-custom-id"); nats_request("rpc.x", "...", 200); wins -- the auto-stage observes the existing entry and steps aside.

Disabling the header. Setting the modparam to the empty string ("") suppresses the auto-stage without affecting the mint: the id is still generated and the $nats_request_id pvar still works, but no header is added to the outbound msg. Use this when you want client-side correlation but the responder cannot accept (or will reject) the extra header.
Range: any string that is a valid NATS header name. Common choices include X-Request-Id, Request-Id, X-Correlation-Id, or a vendor-prefixed X-MyOrg-Request-Id.

example.title: End-to-end correlation: script -- broker -- responder
Vendor-prefixed header for a specific responder service
example.programlisting: modparam("nats_consumer", "request_id_header", "X-MyOrg-Trace-Id")

example.title: Mint id locally but do not put it on the wire

section.title: NATS transport parameters (owned by event_nats)
section.para: Connection-level NATS parameters (nats_url, reconnect_wait, max_reconnect, nats_drain_timeout_ms) are owned by event_nats and read through the shared lib/nats connection pool. Set them on the event_nats module; the consumer reuses the pool directly.

TLS configuration is sourced from the tls_mgm client domain named "nats" at connect time — see the event_nats admin guide "TLS configuration" section for the canonical pattern. nats_consumer declares tls_mgm as DEP_SILENT, so plaintext-only deployments don't need it.

Pull a single JetStream message for the handle id off the SHM ring. Populates the per-worker $nats_* pseudo-variables with the delivered message's subject, data, sequence, delivery count, pending count, reply subject, and headers. Does not acknowledge the message — call nats_ack(), nats_nak(), or nats_term() explicitly.

listitem.para: id (string) — a previously- bound consumer handle id.
timeout_ms (integer, optional) — block up to this many milliseconds waiting for a message. Default 0 = non-blocking.

section.para: Return values: 1 on success, 0 on empty-after-timeout, -1 on handle-not-found or transient error, -2 when the underlying NATS connection is down (the consumer process surfaces this so scripts can distinguish transport failure from empty ring; call nats_last_error() for the human string).
Also exported as an async command; see .

Non-blocking variant of nats_fetch() for use inside async(...) launcher statements. The worker yields immediately after the call and is resumed by the ring's eventfd when a message arrives, or by a reactor timer when timeout_ms expires.

example.title: Async fetch + ack in a request route

section.para: Drain up to count messages in a single call. The fetched messages are staged in a per-worker batch buffer. Use nats_batch_select(i) to activate each one, then ack / nak / term it before moving on.

opts is a semicolon-separated k=v string recognizing count (default 16), expires (duration, default 100ms; accepts a bare integer interpreted as ms, or a suffix of ms / s / m / h / d), expires_ms (alias for expires; bare integer ms), and no_wait (bool, default 0).

Returns the number of messages staged in $retcode, or a negative error code identical to nats_fetch(). The OpenSIPS script does not allow direct assignment of a function return to a $var(); capture $retcode immediately after the call.

When expires is positive, the call waits in 5 ms ticks until the requested count is reached or the deadline expires. This wait is bounded by the worker's request-route processing budget; do not set expires longer than the request a route is meant to take.

example.title: High-throughput batch drain pattern

section.para: Activate the idx-th message in the worker's current batch so subsequent $nats_* reads and nats_ack() / nats_nak() / nats_term() calls target it. Valid indices are 0 .. n-1 where n is the return of the most recent nats_fetch_batch(). Calling nats_batch_select() twice with the same index after acknowledging is a no-op and returns -1.

section.title: Acknowledgement functions

listitem.para: nats_ack() — acknowledge the current message successfully.
nats_ack_next() — ack the current message and immediately request another delivery (reduces round-trips when tight-looping a durable consumer).
nats_ack_progress() — refresh the broker's ack timer without terminating the delivery. Use when a handler must exceed the handle's ack_wait without triggering a redelivery.
nats_nak() — negative-ack the current message; the broker schedules redelivery using the consumer's ack_wait.
nats_nak_delay(delay_ms) — same as nats_nak() but the broker schedules redelivery for delay_ms milliseconds in the future instead of using the consumer's ack_wait.
nats_term() — terminate the delivery with no retry. max_deliver-style advisory semantics still apply on the server side.

nats_in_progress() — like nats_ack_progress(), refresh the broker's ack timer to keep the current delivery alive past ack_wait, but without clearing the worker's current-message state. Subsequent nats_ack() / nats_nak() / nats_term() calls still target the same delivery. Use when a long handler will continue to poll progress and eventually finalise with one of the standard acks.

section.para: All seven return 1 on success or -1 when there is no current message (or the worker's ack token has already been consumed).

Stage a header on the worker-local outgoing header buffer. The next nats_reply() or nats_request() flushes the staged headers onto the outbound NATS message. Up to 16 headers can be staged per worker; attempts to add more return -1.
Staged headers are cleared after every nats_reply() or nats_request() regardless of outcome.

Publish payload to the current message's reply_to subject. Useful as the responder side of a request/reply exchange. Returns 1 on success, -1 when there is no current message, no reply_to, or the publish failed.
NATS request/reply. Publishes payload to subject and waits for a reply on a per-call inbox. Two call shapes:

listitem.para: Sync: nats_request("rpc.x", $var(p), 200); Blocks the calling worker for up to timeout_ms milliseconds. Default route mask permits STARTUP_ROUTE, TIMER_ROUTE, LOCAL_ROUTE, EVENT_ROUTE, ONREPLY_ROUTE only; request_route / failure_route / branch_route / error_route are reachable only after setting allow_sync_anywhere=1.

Async: async(nats_request("rpc.x", $var(p), 200), on_reply); Yields the worker to the reactor on a per-call eventfd. A libnats subscription thread receives the reply on the worker's persistent inbox subscription and signals the eventfd; the resume route runs with the per-worker current-message state populated.

section.para: On success the reply populates the script-visible state: $nats_data, $nats_subject, $nats_hdr(Name), $nats_reply_to. The $nats_request_id pvar holds the UUIDv7 used as the correlation id and is also staged as the X-Request-Id outbound header (modparam-configurable; can be overridden by the script).

Return codes. No path returns 0 -- a 0 return from a script-callable cmd sets ACT_FL_EXIT in run_action_list (action.c:196) and silently terminates the calling route, which is never correct for an RPC result. Every outcome is therefore a positive success code or a negative error / non-result code:

listitem.para: 1 — reply delivered.
-1 — timeout: the broker is still reachable but the responder did not reply within timeout_ms. Script may retry with a longer timeout.

-2 — connection lost mid-flight. Async only. The pool reconnect epoch advanced or nats_pool_is_connected went false during the call. This is distinct from a timeout: the script should treat -2 as broker-down (alert, circuit-break, surface 503) rather than retry-with-longer-tmo.

Latency note. The resume function discovers the lost-connection state when the async-core timer next wakes it; the worst-case time to surface -2 is therefore bounded by the script's timeout_ms. Proactive sub-second reaping requires either a periodic-poll route or a lib/nats disconnect- callback chain; both remain TODO.
-3 — NATS unavailable at issue time (no pool connection). Returned before any publish attempt; the script can short-circuit without paying the timeout_ms wait.
-4 — request error (subject empty / too long, msg create failed, publish or sync-request failed).
-5 — per-worker in-flight cap reached. Async only. Default cap is 4096 simultaneous in-flight calls per worker; callers should implement back-pressure when this surfaces.
-6 — internal error (oom, format failure, missing eventfd, etc.).

example.title: Async request with full error-branch handling

listitem.para: $nats_subject — NATS subject the current message was delivered on.
$nats_data — message payload.
$nats_reply_to — reply subject (empty string when the publisher did not request a reply).
$nats_seq — JetStream stream sequence number.
$nats_consumer_seq — JetStream consumer-side sequence number.
$nats_delivered — broker's delivered counter (1 on first delivery, bumps on each redelivery).
$nats_pending — broker's estimate of messages still waiting on the stream after this one.
$nats_token — opaque ack token encoding (handle_idx, slot_idx, generation).
$nats_hdr(Name) — read a header from the current message. Lookup is case-insensitive. Name must be a literal; dynamic $nats_hdr($var(x)) forms are not supported.

$nats_request_id (read/write) — UUIDv7 correlation id of the most recent nats_request issued by this worker. Minted automatically by both the sync and async start paths at call time and stashed in per-worker process memory so it is readable before the call returns (sync), across the async() yield, and inside the resume route. Returns NULL on workers that have not yet issued any nats_request call and have no pending script assignment.

Same value is auto-staged as the X-Request-Id outbound header (header name configurable via the request_id_header modparam); responders that echo the header on the reply get end-to-end correlation across logs and traces. Set the modparam to an empty string to mint but not stage.

Caller-set override. Assigning to the pvar before a nats_request call swaps the auto-minted UUIDv7 for the script-supplied value on that one call (consume-once semantics): subsequent calls with no further assignment revert to minting fresh. Useful for propagating an inbound trace id (e.g. from a SIP header, an upstream tracing context, or a callee-specific correlation token) into the NATS request so the entire trace shares one id end-to-end.

Validation: 1..63 bytes, no CR/LF (would inject pseudo-headers into the NATS wire format). Out-of-range assignments are rejected, an LM_WARN is logged explaining why, and the next call falls back to minting a fresh UUIDv7.

Clearing the override. Both $nats_request_id = NULL and $nats_request_id = "" clear the pending override and the stash. The NULL form is handled by the pvar setter's early-return fast path; the empty-string form falls through to the same clear routine via the len <= 0 branch. After a clear, $nats_request_id reads NULL until the next nats_request mints fresh.

example.title: Correlate an async RPC across log lines
Propagate an inbound SIP X-Trace-Id into the NATS RPC

section.para: Register a consumer handle. Takes a single config parameter: a semicolon-separated k=v string. Required keys: id, stream, and either durable=<name> or ephemeral=1. Optional keys cover the full JetStream pull-consumer matrix: filter, filters, deliver_policy, start_seq, start_time, replay_policy, ack_policy, ack_wait, max_deliver, backoff, max_ack_pending, headers_only, sample_freq, rate_limit, inactive_threshold, js_domain, api_prefix, ring_capacity.
Unknown keys are preserved in the handle's extra_json for forward-compat and survive persistence round-trips.

example.title: Register a durable work-queue consumer
example.programlisting: opensips-cli -x mi nats_consumer_bind \ 'id=jobs;stream=WORK;durable=jobs_worker;filter=work.jobs;ack_wait=30s;max_deliver=5'

section.para: Remove a handle by id. The handle is marked retired immediately so subsequent lookups miss; the consumer process tears down the JetStream subscription on its next iteration, and the registry reaper frees the handle once all in-flight ack operations drain. nats_consumer_unbind always returns promptly — the deferred teardown is invisible to the caller.

example.title: Unbind a handle
example.programlisting: opensips-cli -x mi nats_consumer_unbind id=jobs

section.para: List all registered handles with their bind-time configuration and current runtime counters (pulls_requested, msgs_delivered, acks, naks, terms, redeliveries, last_error_code, last_error_msg).
Each handle entry also carries two backoff-state fields that surface the worker's ensure_subscription_for_handle() retry budget:

listitem.para: ensure_failures — consecutive ensure-subscription failures. Zero on a healthy handle; non-zero while the worker is in backoff after the broker rejected js_AddConsumer / js_PullSubscribe (e.g. the broker-side durable was deleted out from under &osips;, or the underlying stream is missing). The next failed retry would push the value to seven, at which point the backoff saturates at the 60 s cap and a one-shot WARN names the handle.
ensure_next_retry_at — unix timestamp (CLOCK_REALTIME seconds) before which the worker will skip this handle's reconcile tick. Zero means "retry on the next tick".

section.para: Both fields reset to zero on the first successful resubscribe; a corresponding INFO ("recovered after N failed ensure attempt(s)") fires once per recovery edge so log forwarders can tell wedge from recovery without polling.

Re-read the persistence file (persist_path) and merge in any handles that are not already bound. Existing handles are untouched — the reload is additive. Returns the number of new handles loaded. Useful when the snapshot has been hand-edited or copied in from another node.

Liveness snapshot of the dedicated consumer process. Intended for external watchdogs (Prometheus exporters, Nagios checks, etc.) -- a wedged or crashed consumer leaves SIP workers blocked on the per-handle SHM ring's eventfd with no other observable signal.
Returns a JSON object:

listitem.para: tick — monotonic loop-iteration counter incremented once per consumer-loop pass.
consumer_pid — PID of the consumer process.
last_tick_ms_ago — Milliseconds since the last tick (CLOCK_MONOTONIC).
stale — "true" when last_tick_ms_ago exceeds 5000 ms (more than five times the loop's 1 s upper-wait bound) or no tick has been observed yet.

example.title: Poll consumer health from opensips-cli
example.programlisting: $ opensips-cli -x mi nats_consumer_health { "tick": 12943, "consumer_pid": 4711, "last_tick_ms_ago": 821, "stale": "false" }

section.title: Durable Work Queue
section.para: Two or more &osips; instances binding the same durable name on the same stream load-balance deliveries via JetStream's consumer machinery. No duplicates unless nats_nak()/ack_wait expiry triggers a redelivery.

example.title: startup_route + timer_route drain loop

section.title: Ephemeral Advisory Subscriber
section.para: JetStream advisories ($JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.>) are a natural fit for an ephemeral consumer — the subscription lives only while &osips; does, and inactive_threshold keeps the broker tidy if the process dies unexpectedly.

example.title: Subscribe to max-delivery advisories

section.title: Request / Reply
section.para: The responder side binds a durable consumer on a stream covering the request subject; the requester side calls nats_request() from a timer_route or startup_route.

example.title: Two-route RPC on the same instance
